agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A new feature request introduces a 'confirmed' status for quotes, but the term 'confirmed' is already established in the order domain with a fundamentally different meaning. In orders, 'confirmed' means paid and immutable. In quotes, 'confirmed' would mean verbally agreed but unpaid and still mutable. This is a direct collision in the ubiquitous language.","bounded_context":"Quote Lifecycle vs Order Fulfillment — two contexts attempting to share the same term with contradictory semantics","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Confirmed Order","definition":"An order that has been paid and is queued for fulfillment. It is immutable — no further changes are permitted."},{"term":"Confirmed Quote (proposed)","definition":"A quote where the customer has verbally agreed to terms but payment has NOT occurred. Implicitly still mutable (terms could change before payment)."},{"term":"Quote","definition":"A priced proposal presented to a customer, subject to acceptance and eventual conversion to an order."},{"term":"Immutable","definition":"Cannot be changed after reaching this state. A hard invariant on confirmed orders."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"A confirmed order has been paid and is immutable — no modifications are permitted after confirmation.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Immutability after payment is a data integrity constraint. Violating it could cause financial discrepancies between what was paid and what is fulfilled.","testable_assertion":"Given an order in 'confirmed' status, any attempt to modify its line items, pricing, or terms MUST be rejected."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"The term 'confirmed' in the order domain implies payment has occurred.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Payment is the precondition that triggers confirmation. If 'confirmed' can exist without payment (as in the quote proposal), the invariant is semantically violated across contexts.","testable_assertion":"Every entity in 'confirmed' status MUST have an associated completed payment — or the term 'confirmed' has lost its meaning."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"A quote moves to an accepted/agreed state when a customer verbally agrees to terms, prior to payment.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a business workflow policy — the specific name and conditions of this status are a policy decision, not a physical or data-integrity constraint.","testable_assertion":"Given a quote in draft/pending status, when a sales agent records customer verbal agreement, then the quote transitions to an accepted state and is distinguishable from a paid, immutable order."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"The ubiquitous language already defines 'confirmed' as paid + immutable in the order context","when":"A new status is introduced for quotes representing verbal agreement without payment","then":"The new status MUST use a distinct term (e.g., 'accepted', 'agreed', 'verbally_committed') to avoid semantic collision with the established 'confirmed' meaning"},{"id":"AC-2","given":"A quote has received verbal customer agreement","when":"A sales agent records this agreement in the system","then":"The quote transitions to the new pre-payment status and remains mutable until payment is received and an order is created"},{"id":"AC-3","given":"Any entity in the system is in 'confirmed' status","when":"A developer or downstream system queries that status","then":"It MUST unambiguously mean the entity is paid and immutable — there must be no second interpretation of 'confirmed'"}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"A developer writes code that checks `status == 'confirmed'` across both quotes and orders, assuming identical semantics","expected_behavior":"This query would conflate unpaid quotes with paid orders, causing incorrect fulfillment triggers or financial reporting errors. The term collision makes this bug inevitable."},{"scenario":"A reporting dashboard aggregates all 'confirmed' entities to forecast revenue","expected_behavior":"Verbally-agreed quotes (no payment) would be counted alongside paid orders, inflating confirmed revenue figures."},{"scenario":"A customer verbally agrees (quote 'confirmed') but then disputes terms before payment","expected_behavior":"Under the order definition, 'confirmed' is immutable. If quote 'confirmed' inherits that assumption, the system may prevent legitimate pre-payment modifications to the quote."},{"scenario":"An integration or API consumer receives a 'confirmed' webhook and triggers fulfillment","expected_behavior":"If the webhook fires for a quote confirmation (no payment), goods could be shipped without payment. The semantic collision creates a fulfillment-before-payment risk."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"High risk. If 'confirmed' is overloaded, downstream systems may treat unpaid verbal agreements as paid commitments, leading to premature fulfillment, revenue misstatement, or bad-debt exposure.","operational":"Significant. Every system, report, and integration that keys on 'confirmed' status must now disambiguate between two meanings. This increases complexity and bug surface area permanently.","user":"Sales agents need a way to record verbal agreement — the business need is valid. But the naming choice creates confusion for anyone who interacts with both quotes and orders.","risk":"Term collision is a classic source of domain corruption. It introduces ambiguity that compounds over time as more features are built on the overloaded term. This is a high-severity domain drift risk.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Sales Agents","impact":"Need the ability to record verbal agreement — legitimate need, but the status name must not collide with order semantics"},{"group":"Finance / Accounting","impact":"Any reporting that filters on 'confirmed' status will conflate paid orders with unpaid verbal agreements, risking inaccurate financial statements"},{"group":"Fulfillment / Warehouse","impact":"If fulfillment triggers on 'confirmed', unpaid quotes could accidentally enter the fulfillment pipeline"},{"group":"Engineering / Integration Partners","impact":"API consumers and internal services that react to 'confirmed' status changes will need to handle two incompatible meanings, increasing bug risk"},{"group":"Customers","impact":"Indirect — risk of premature fulfillment or incorrect invoicing if the term collision causes downstream errors"}]},"confidence":{"level":40,"reasoning":"The spec contains a direct contradiction: the established domain defines 'confirmed' as paid + immutable, while the feature request proposes 'confirmed' to mean unpaid + mutable. Both are explicit statements from the business. This is not ambiguity — it is two incompatible definitions for the same term. Confidence is low because shipping this as-is would corrupt the ubiquitous language.","high_confidence_areas":["The existing definition of 'confirmed order' is clear and established","The business need for recording verbal agreement on quotes is valid","The two definitions of 'confirmed' are semantically incompatible"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether the requesting team is aware of the existing 'confirmed' definition in orders","What the preferred alternative term should be — this requires stakeholder alignment"],"assumptions":["The existing 'confirmed order' definition is authoritative and in active use across the system","Quotes and orders share infrastructure where status terms could collide (APIs, databases, reporting)"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"The term 'confirmed' is already defined in the order domain as 'paid and immutable.' The feature request redefines 'confirmed' for quotes to mean 'verbally agreed, unpaid, and implicitly mutable.' These two definitions directly contradict each other. Using the same term for both will corrupt the ubiquitous language and cause downstream bugs in fulfillment, reporting, and integrations.","affected_stakeholders":["Sales Agents","Finance","Fulfillment","Engineering","Integration Partners"],"options":["Rename the quote status to 'accepted' (customer accepted terms, payment pending)","Rename the quote status to 'agreed' (verbal agreement recorded)","Rename the quote status to 'verbally_committed' (explicit about the nature of the commitment)","Keep 'confirmed' for quotes and accept the semantic collision (NOT recommended)"],"recommendation":"Rename the quote status to 'accepted'. It accurately describes the business event (customer accepted terms), clearly distinguishes from 'confirmed' (which means paid + immutable), and is intuitive for sales agents. The underlying business need is valid — only the naming must change."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Reuse of the term 'confirmed' with a contradictory definition (unpaid + mutable vs paid + immutable)","business_rule_broken":"BR-1, BR-2: 'Confirmed' is an established invariant meaning paid and immutable. Overloading it to mean unpaid and mutable in the quote context violates the ubiquitous language and risks corrupting downstream systems that depend on the unambiguous meaning of 'confirmed'."}]}
